topic: Philosophy of Religion: The Nature & Influence of Religious Experience
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Does saying an experience is indescribable make it meaningless?
items.correct: No - ineffability is typical of profound experiences such as love or music; James lists it as a characteristic mark of mysticism, not a reason for dismissal.
items.misconception: If someone cannot fully describe an experience, it cannot mean anything.
items.random_wrong: Ineffability means the experience happened in a foreign language.
items.why: Students turn a defining feature into a defeater; the philosophical question is what limited language can still convey.

items.q: Does Swinburne's principle of credulity force us to accept every claim?
items.correct: No - it is defeasible: special considerations such as intoxication, known unreliability or conflicting evidence can override the presumption of trust.
items.misconception: Swinburne says we must believe every reported religious experience without question.
items.random_wrong: Swinburne argued that religious experiences never happen.
items.why: The principle creates a starting presumption, not blind acceptance; missing the defeasibility makes it look absurd.

items.q: Are all conversions sudden and dramatic like Paul's?
items.correct: No - James distinguished sudden conversions from gradual, volitional ones in which a person slowly builds a new spiritual identity.
items.misconception: A real conversion must be an instant, dramatic event.
items.random_wrong: Conversions only happened in the first century.
items.why: The Damascus road dominates the imagination; James's typology shows the quieter pattern is just as significant.

items.correct: An encounter with an awe-inspiring, fascinating mystery that is 'wholly other'
items.misconception: A feeling of mild happiness in worship
items.random_wrong: The ability to predict the future
items.why: Correct answer: An encounter with an awe-inspiring, fascinating mystery that is 'wholly other'. “A feeling of mild happiness in worship” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does Swinburne's principle of credulity claim?
items.correct: Experiences should normally be trusted as they seem, unless there are special grounds for doubt
items.misconception: No experience should ever be trusted
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Experiences should normally be trusted as they seem, unless there are special grounds for doubt. “No experience should ever be trusted” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which of these is an example of a corporate religious experience?
items.misconception: Paul's conversion on the Damascus road
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The Toronto Blessing. “Paul's conversion on the Damascus road” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How did Freud explain religious experience?
items.correct: As wish-fulfilment - an illusion arising from deep psychological needs
items.random_wrong: As a symptom of physical fitness
items.why: It's As wish-fulfilment - an illusion arising from deep psychological needs. “As genuine contact with God” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
